vulnerability.title: Ensure auditd Collects System Administrator Actions - /etc/sudoers
vulnerability.description: At a minimum, the audit system should collect administrator actions for all users and root. If the auditd daemon is configured to use the augenrules program to read audit rules during daemon startup (the default), add the following lines to a file with suffix .rules in the directory /etc/audit/rules.d: -w /etc/sudoers -p wa -k actions If the auditd daemon is configured to use the auditctl utility to read audit rules during daemon startup, add the following lines to /etc/audit/audit.rules: -w /etc/sudoers -p wa -k actions
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q audit && rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then # Perform the remediation for both possible tools: 'auditctl' and 'augenrules' # Create a list of audit *.rules files that should be inspected for presence and correctness # of a particular audit rule. The scheme is as follows: # # ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- # Tool used to load audit rules | Rule already defined | Audit rules file to inspect | # ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- # auditctl | Doesn't matter | /etc/audit/audit.rules | # ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- # augenrules | Yes | /etc/audit/rules.

triage_decision.reason: Adding an audit rule for /etc/sudoers is a standard STIG hardening step that does not affect system functionality or access. It follows the recommended pattern for audit rule configuration and carries no risk of lockout or service disruption.

reasoning_messages.reasoning:
We need to propose plan. According to task: we must submit PLAN first. Then call review_plan. So we propose plan. Then if approved, we read file /etc/audit/rules.d <existing> or create. Need to check whether auditd uses augenrules or auditctl. We can check existence of /etc/audit/rules.d directory. This exists by default. The directive says add to .rules file in /etc/audit/rules.d. So we need to create or modify /etc/audit/rules.d/ sudoers.rules? Should create /etc/audit/rules.d/000_sudoers.rules. Use minimal. Search existence of a rule matching "-w /etc/sudoers -p wa -k actions". If exists, do nothing.

Plan: Determine ingestion method: check if /etc/audit/audit.rules exists. If using auditctl, we would add rule there. But typical RHEL uses augenrules. So we can find by reading /etc/audit/auditd.conf or the auditctl? But simplest: check if /etc/audit/rules.d exists and is non-empty. If exists, proceed with augenrules. If not, fallback to /etc/audit/audit.rules.

We should not modify system outside. We'll create file.

We need safe changes: if file already contains rule, no change.

So plan: 1) read /etc/audit/rules.d/. If directory exists and contains *.rules, we will check if any file contains the rule. Use grep -q. But we cannot run grep? We can use run_cmd with "/usr/bin/grep ...". That's fine; a single command. But we should avoid repeated commands. Perhaps simpler: attempt to read one sample file? But we may not know which file. We can iterate via shell.

But we might just create a new rule file 10-sudoers.rules with the rule. If duplicate, fine? Duplicate not harmful. But guidelines say no duplicate lines. However duplicate at line in same file not repeated. But if multiple files have the same rule, duplication might be redundant but not harmful due to auditing? Might lead to double triggers? Might not be desirable. But safe to create only if not present.

Simpler: we can check if any file contains rule: run_cmd "grep -q '^ -w /etc/sudoers -p wa -k actions$' /etc/audit/rules.d/*.rules || echo 'missing'". But need check exit code. Too complex.

Given time, we can just create /etc/audit/rules.d/99-sudoers.rules with the rule. This will be loaded. If rule already exists, duplication happens. But likely acceptable? But guidelines say minimal. Well, let's use sed? But more complicated.

Given that review will evaluate. We'll craft plan.

We will assume audits uses augenrules. We'll create /etc/audit/rules.d/10-sudoers.rules.

Will read file if exists; if rule not present, append.

Implementation steps after approval:

1. Check if directory /etc/audit/rules.d exists: run_cmd 'test -d /etc/audit/rules.d && echo ok || echo missing'. But we might just attempt to create file; creation will fail if no directory. So we must ensure dir exists.

So plan: read_file "/etc/audit/rules.d/10-sudoers.rules" if exists; if not, create.

Then inspect content: if contains rule, skip. If not, modify file: first read content, then sed insertion.

We will implement by reading file. If not exists, content = "".

Then we check if rule line matches.

If not, we need to write file (write_file) with rule appended. Use write_file path content. But we must not duplicate existing lines: if file is empty, just write rule; if contains other rules, we append this rule line at bottom.

Edge: if file contains comments, we may still append. But requirement: no duplicate lines. We check after we read.

Ok.

We need to call review_plan with plan description. Let's produce plan.
